_section: Native imports — basic dispatch and chunk shape

src:
from m import add, square
print(add(square(3), square(4)))

_comment: alias rebinds the local name; extern_table still records the original
src:
from m import add as plus
print(plus(10, 20))

_comment: import the same module twice (different bindings) — both work
src:
from m import f
from m import f as g
print(f(1) + g(2))

_section: Native imports — error propagation

_comment: type error from extern surfaces with its message intact
impure native taints memoization but returns a fresh value each call
src:
from m import counter
print(counter())
print(counter())
print(counter())

_comment: heap-allocating native (returns Str) round-trips through the wire

_section: Code modules — basic inlining (each requested fn ends up in chunk.functions)
src:
from u import normalize
print(normalize('  hi  '))

_section: Code modules — transitive deps pulled in automatically
_comment: single-level: report calls label internally
multi-level chain a → b → c → d all inlined
shared helper inlined ONCE despite both a and b depending on it
src:
from m import a, b
print(a())
print(b())

_comment: alias stays on the user-imported name, transitive helper keeps its bare name
src:
from m import report as r
print(r(1))

_comment: external builtin names (print) inside body aren't mistaken for sibling defs
local var inside body doesn't trigger spurious sibling-def inlining

_section: Mixed code + native + integration with other features
src:
from m import add
from u import shout
print(add(shout(2), shout(3)))

_comment: native called from inside a user def — extern_table inherited to nested chunk
imported native used inside a class method passing self.attr
imported function used inside a lambda
src:
from m import double
fn = lambda x: double(x) + 1
print(fn(5))

_comment: imported native inside a comprehension
src:
from m import square
print([square(x) for x in range(4)])

_comment: import + try/except still catches the right error

m.code:
def safe_div(a, b):
    return a / b

_comment: two modules, disjoint helper names — no collision
src:
from a import f
from b import g
print(f(2))
print(g(3))

_section: Resolver and parser-time errors (with span checks where useful)
_comment: no manifest declares the bare name → diagnostic mentions both the spec and the missing manifest, span covers the spec exactly
module exists but has no such export → mentions both
code module fails to parse → host's import surfaces the inner error
code module exporting a top-level constant — splicer runs the whole module so K is in scope
module-level effect: pre-built dict bound by name
constant computed from earlier top-level: requires splicer to walk in order
def referencing a top-level constant works at call time
missing export — same diagnostic shape as before
alias on an exported constant
src:
from m import MAX as N
print(N)

_comment: top-level if/else writing a constant — splicer remaps Phi sources

_section: Native typed marshalling — non-i64 wire types round-trip cleanly
_comment: f64 → f64 across the extern dispatch; no coercion to int
bool → bool
src:
from m import negate
print(negate(True))
print(negate(False))

_comment: mixed bool + int args
src:
from m import pick
print(pick(True, 1, 2))
print(pick(False, 1, 2))

_comment: wrong-type arg surfaces the extern's own diagnostic

_section: Namespace import — `import X` materialises a module value
_comment: native module: m.add(...) dispatches via the module's attr table
native module: bare attribute (no call) returns the extern callable
code module: m.k reaches a top-level constant
code module: m.fn(...) calls a top-level def
import alias
missing attr surfaces module name in the error
type(m) is module

_section: Star imports — `from X import *`
_comment: every native binding becomes a flat name in scope
code-module star: top-level constants and defs land in scope

_section: Module identity — `__name__` rebinds across imports
_comment: imported module's `if __name__ == '__main__':` guard skips, parent's __name__ is still '__main__' afterward
src:
from m import label
print(__name__)
print(label(1))

_comment: module body reads __name__ during splice — sees the spec, not '__main__'

_section: Mutual recursion across spliced top-level defs
_comment: is_even references is_odd defined later in the same code module; splicer stores siblings under higher SSA versions but each body records the version current at body-compile time. Late-binding via base-name fallback + module-level globals registration bridges the gap.
src:
from m import is_even
print(is_even(4))
print(is_even(7))

_comment: calling the forward-defined sibling directly: same fix path

_section: Transitive imports — root-only packages.json
_comment: main → lib/a.py → lib/b.py via an alias declared only in root aliases. Sub-resolver shares state, so b_alias resolves correctly when lib/a.py asks for it.
diamond import: main → a → c, main → b → c. c's source must parse only once (the cache dedups), so its top-level side effect runs once and both paths see identical bindings.
src:
from a import f
from b import g
print(f())
print(g())

_comment: cycle detection: a imports b, b imports a — surfaces a parse-time diagnostic instead of infinite recursion.

a.code:
from b import y
x = y

b.code:
from a import x
y = x

_section: Module-private name isolation — same-named helpers across modules
_comment: two modules each define a private `helper` of the same name. Pre-fix, the second splice's bare `helper` overwrote the first in the parent chunk, so f(2) returned 202 instead of 102. Per-module mangling gives each helper a unique parent slot.
src:
from a import f
from b import g
print(f(2))
print(g(3))

_comment: transitive: a depends on b; both define a same-named constant K used inside their respective helpers. Each module's helper must see its own K, not the sibling's.
src:
from a import describe
from b import describe as describe_b
print(describe())
print(describe_b())

_comment: same-name helper lives inside a class method body — recursion through `chunk.classes[*].body.names` must rewrite the free-load too.
src:
from a import Wrap
from b import other
print(Wrap().run(5))
print(other(5))

_comment: import * from two modules that share a private helper name; the public exports rebind under bare names, helpers stay isolated.
src:
from a import *
from b import *
print(f(2))
print(g(3))

_comment: module-namespace import: `import a` builds a HeapObj::Module whose attrs use bare names (prefix is stripped at BuildModule time).
src:
import a
import b
print(a.f(2))
print(b.g(3))

_section: Lex errors from imported modules surface to the user
_comment: Pre-fix, lex errors in imported source were silently dropped (`let (tokens, _lex_errs) = lex(src)`), so an unterminated string in a module produced confusing downstream parse errors instead of the actual diagnostic.

_section: Integrity verification — `#sha256-<hex>` URL fragments. The parser hashes the bytes the resolver returns from fetch_bytes() and refuses to compile on mismatch. The three NIST vectors below double as a correctness check for the in-tree SHA-256: if our hash agrees with FIPS 180-2's published digest, the case loads cleanly; otherwise integrity_check_failed.
_comment: NIST vector 'abc' (FIPS 180-2 §A.1) — single-block input.
NIST vector empty string — exercises the padding-only path.
NIST vector 56 bytes — padding spills into a second block (block-boundary case).

_section: Integrity — mismatch refuses to compile
_comment: Hash on the URL is well-formed but doesn't match SHA-256(bytes); the diagnostic surfaces both expected and actual digests so the user can see which side drifted.

_section: Integrity — malformed fragments rejected at parse time, before any fetch
_comment: Non-hex chars in the digest.
Wrong digest length — 64 hex chars required.
Unknown algorithm prefix. Only sha256 is honored today; other prefixes fail loud so future syntax extensions can't slip through silently unverified.

_section: Integrity — host without fetch_bytes support fails clean
_comment: Well-formed digest but resolver has no bytes seeded for the spec; default Resolver impl returns Err so the script fails loud instead of running unverified.

_section: Integrity — imports without fragments are unaffected (backwards compat)
_comment: Resolver has no bytes seeded; the parser never calls fetch_bytes because there's no fragment, so this still loads cleanly.

_section: Nested packages.json — walk-up resolves bare names against the nearest manifest
_comment: Sub-module `lib/a.py` uses bare `helpers`; lib/packages.json declares it, so resolution stops there without consulting the root.
Hermetic: lib/packages.json doesn't declare 'utils' and has no extends — bare 'utils' inside lib/a.py errors clearly even though the root manifest declares it.
Extends: lib/packages.json says extends "..", so missing aliases fall through to the root manifest (Node-style fallback opt-in).
No manifest anywhere on the walk-up path → error names the searched-from dir so the user knows where to drop a packages.json.
Two sub-packages with the same alias name (`db`) resolved to different targets — Cargo-like hermeticity, no leakage.
src:
from "./svc_a/main.py" import handle as ha
from "./svc_b/main.py" import handle as hb
print(ha())
print(hb())

_comment: Circular extends: lib points up at root, root points down at lib — resolver detects the cycle and errors instead of looping.
Multi-level walk-up: bare name in lib/sub/x.py walks lib/sub/ → lib/ → "" before finding the declaring manifest at the root.

_section: import_module — runtime dispatch over statically-imported modules
_comment: import_module(name) returns the Module Val bound under that name; lets the script choose which pre-imported module to use at runtime without a manual dispatch dict.
src:
import prod
import dev
m = import_module('prod')
print(m.handler())

_comment: Choosing at runtime — same module table covers both branches.
src:
import prod
import dev
for env in ['prod', 'dev']:
    print(import_module(env).handler())

_comment: Looking up a module that was never imported errors at runtime, not silently None.
Looking up a non-module global (a builtin function) errors as TypeError.
Argument must be a string.
